topic: Theme 2: Political and Artistic Culture in the Spanish-speaking World
subject: spanish

items.q: Is flamenco just a style of dance?
items.correct: No - flamenco is a threefold art of cante (song), toque (guitar) and baile (dance), held together by the compás.
items.misconception: Flamenco only means the dancing with dresses and castanets.
items.random_wrong: Flamenco is a Mexican mariachi style.
items.why: Reducing flamenco to dance ignores the centrality of the singer and guitarist; essays on Spanish artistic culture need the full three-part definition.

items.q: Is el Día de los Muertos simply the Mexican version of Halloween?
items.correct: No - it is a distinct tradition honouring deceased family members with altars, food and flowers, blending indigenous and Catholic elements.
items.misconception: It is just Halloween with different decorations.
items.random_wrong: It is a national sports holiday.
items.why: The two festivals fall close together, but their purposes differ: remembrance and family connection rather than fright and trick-or-treating.

items.q: Does 'una fiesta' only mean a private party?
items.correct: No - it also means a public festival or holiday, such as las fiestas de un pueblo or una fiesta nacional.
items.misconception: Fiesta always refers to a birthday-style party.
items.random_wrong: Fiesta is a word used only for weddings.
items.why: Texts on Hispanic culture constantly use 'fiesta' in the festival sense; the narrow reading distorts comprehension of articles about traditions.
Correct answer: Andalusia, in southern Spain. “Patagonia” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: El Día de los Muertos is celebrated mainly in which country, and when?
items.why: It's Mexico, on 1-2 November. “Spain, in July” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: A tomato-throwing festival held in Buñol
items.why: It's A tomato-throwing festival held in Buñol. “A flamenco competition in Seville” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Reggaetón has its roots in which places?
items.why: Correct answer: Panama and Puerto Rico. “Madrid and Barcelona” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
